title: This Was a Big Week for Marie Curie, More Than 120 Years Ago
author: Bob Grant
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementHistoryThis Was a Big Week for Marie Curie, More Than 120 Years AgoDespite steep odds, she became the first woman in France to earn a doctorate in scienceBGBy Bob Grant4:00 PM CDT on June 26, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyMarie Curie is an icon of science. She was not only the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, in 1903, she is still the only person to win two science Nobels in different fields—her 1903 win was in Physics while her 1911 medal was in Chemistry. But before either of those honors (but not long before) she notched another first. She was the first woman in France to be awarded a doctorate in the sciences. That accomplishment came on June 25, 1903, a mere six months before she would win her first Nobel Prize, for the very thesis research that she defended before her graduate committee in Paris that late-June day.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 1070.6K 1‘Ring of Fire’ Eclipse Darkens the Sky Wowing Spectators With ‘Gold Ring’ShareNextStayShe achieved all of these remarkable accomplishments despite the odds being stacked against her. Her family in her native Poland was disadvantaged from the start. There were lost fortunes and properties on both her mother’s and father’s sides after participating in movements to free their homeland from the control of Imperial Russia. After her being born Maria Skłodowska in 1867, Curie had to navigate the sexism that ruled her day. She wasn’t even allowed to attend university in Poland as women were prohibited from pursuing higher education.QUITE THE GRADUATE WORK: Marie Curie's doctoral thesis not only netted her the first such degree ever for a woman in France, it scored her the Nobel Prize.
